coredata.dc:title: Rational design of efficient nanosensor for glyphosate and temperature out of terbium complexes with 1,3-diketone calix[4]arenes

coredata.dc:description: The temperature and substrate dependent luminescence of terbium complexes with calix[4]arene derivatives makes them promising building blocks of nanosensors. Thus, calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones in 1,3-alternate and cone isomeric forms bearing buthyl, dodecyl, nonyl and bromine substituents are introduced as ligands forming luminescent Tb3+ complexes in basified DMF solutions. The structure variation of the ligands allows to highlight buthyl-substituted derivative of calix[4]arene 1,3-diketone in 1,3-alternate isomeric form as the optimal structure. The detailed studies of the complex stoichiometry by means of UV-Vis and H1 NMR diffusion spectroscopy methods reveal 2:2 stoichiometry as the main for the calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones in 1,3-alternate isomeric form. The predominance of 2:2 over 1:1 and 1:2 (Tb:L) complex stoichiometries for the buthyl-substituted ligand was confirmed by comparison of the quantum chemically calculated thermochemical parameters of their complex formation. However, the 2:2 complex formation is either restricted for their dodecyl-substituted derivative or interferes with the 1:2 stoichiometry for the bromo-substituted counterpart. The conversion of the Tb3+ complexes from the DMF solutions to the PSS-stabilized colloids through the solvent-exchange procedure reveals the unfavorable effect of the nonyl- and dodecyl-substituted ligands on aggregation behavior of the colloids. Thus, 2:2 terbium complex with buthyl-substituted ligand in 1,3-alternate isomeric form is the optimal basis for production of the luminescent PSS-colloids with high colloid stability. The Tb3+- luminescence of the colloids exhibit the enhanced sensitivity to both environmental pollutant glyphosate with LOD = 1.97 nM and temperature changes within the physiological temperature range with SI = −4.67 K-% at 323 K.

CTb3 + = CL = 0.1 mM. The UV–Vis spectra of the solutions were recorded after their storage for 3 hours.

CPSS-colloids = 7,5 μM (a–d); CPSS-colloids = 0.75 μM (e and f); CTRIS = 1 mM.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The temperature and substrate dependent luminescence of terbium complexes with calix[4]arene derivatives makes them promising building blocks of nanosensors. Thus, calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones in 1,3-alternate and cone isomeric forms bearing buthyl, dodecyl, nonyl and bromine substituents are introduced as ligands forming luminescent Tb3+ complexes in basified DMF solutions. The structure variation of the ligands allows to highlight buthyl-substituted derivative of calix[4]arene 1,3-diketone in 1,3-alternate isomeric form as the optimal structure. The detailed studies of the complex stoichiometry by means of UV-Vis and H1 NMR diffusion spectroscopy methods reveal 2:2 stoichiometry as the main for the calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones in 1,3-alternate isomeric form. The predominance of 2:2 over 1:1 and 1:2 (Tb:L) complex stoichiometries for the buthyl-substituted ligand was confirmed by comparison of the quantum chemically calculated thermochemical parameters of their complex formation. However, the 2:2 complex formation is either restricted for their dodecyl-substituted derivative or interferes with the 1:2 stoichiometry for the bromo-substituted counterpart. The conversion of the Tb3+ complexes from the DMF solutions to the PSS-stabilized colloids through the solvent-exchange procedure reveals the unfavorable effect of the nonyl- and dodecyl-substituted ligands on aggregation behavior of the colloids. Thus, 2:2 terbium complex with buthyl-substituted ligand in 1,3-alternate isomeric form is the optimal basis for production of the luminescent PSS-colloids with high colloid stability. The Tb3+- luminescence of the colloids exhibit the enhanced sensitivity to both environmental pollutant glyphosate with LOD = 1.97 nM and temperature changes within the physiological temperature range with SI = −4.67 K-% at 323 K.

ce:section.ce:para: Unique photophysical characteristics of lanthanide complexes make them promising basis for design and synthesis of either water soluble or water dispersible sensors and cellular contrast agents [1–10]. The forbiddance of f-f transitions in accordance to the so-called Laporte’s rule results in poor lanthanide-centered luminescence of lanthanide ions itself. However, it can be greatly sensitized through the ligand-to-lanthanide energy transfer [4]. Thus, recognizing an impact of the nanomaterial constructed from lanthanide salts or oxides on developing of water dispersible nanomaterial for sensing or cellular marking [1–10], it is noteworthy that the high luminescence of coordination lanthanide compounds is prospective for analytical or biomedical applications [6,10]. Moreover, the compounds can be spontaneously generated from ligands and lanthanide ions and self-aggregated into nanoparticles in specific solvent and/or concentration conditions [10].

Lanthanide complexes with 1,3-diketone derivatives are very promising for development of highly luminescent nanomaterial for sensing due to efficient ligand-to-metal energy transfer which, in turn, can be tuned through temperature, pH variation or presence of chelating substrates [11–14]. However, design of sensitive sensor to specific substrate requires structure optimization on both molecular and supramolecular levels. Poly-1,3-diketones constructed by embedding of 1,3-diketone moieties onto upper rim of calix[4]arene backbone provide versatile ligands for lanthanide complexes, where structure diversification is considered as powerful tool for tuning of lanthanide-centered luminescence [15–21]. Main advantage of the embedding 1,3-diketone-groups onto calix[4]arene backbone derives from the fact that the latter is still open for substitution by additional functional groups [10,22]. This makes possible to control the ability of the calix[4]arene-based ligands to form lanthanide complexes and to sensitize lanthanide-centered luminescence [23]. Noteworthy, that the impact of the structure variations on the sensing properties of the calix[4]arene-based ligands documented in the literature has been highlighted for the cone conformation [24], while the comparative analysis of the cone and 1,3-alternate-isomers as the ligands for lanthanide-based sensors hasn’t been enough recognized. Thus, the present work introduces newly synthesized tetra- and bis-1,3-diketones of calix[4]arene in the 1,3-alternate isomer form as the ligands for complex formation with Tb3+ ions. The “structure-property” relationship is highlighted by the comparison of both complex structure and sensitizing effects on the Tb3+-centered luminescence for the calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones in the cone and 1,3-alternate isomeric forms. The “structure-property” relationship is of particular impact on controlling of thermally-triggered quenching of Tb3+-centered luminescence, in turn, undergoing through several quenching mechanisms, including a back metal-to-ligand energy transfer. Thus, an impact of the aforesaid isomeric forms of calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones on both thermal sensitivity of their terbium complexes luminescence and quenching mechanisms will be highlighted in the paper.

The conversion of the synthesized complexes into water dispersible hydrophilic colloids exhibiting Tb3+-luminescence will be also represented as a prerequisite for applicability of the produced luminescent complexes for sensing in aqueous media. The sensing properties of the colloids will be represented by the sensitivity of the luminescence to the presence of glyphosate as the well-known representative of the environmental pollutants [25–27]. The ligands structure impact will be highlighted through the comparative analysis of the Tb3+ complexes with calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones in the cone and 1,3-alternate isomeric forms. Noteworthy, that the sensing of glyphosate through lanthanide-centered luminescence has been represented in literature by rather few examples [28,29]. Directed synthesis of novel ligands characterized by different complexation behavior and diverse assembly in colloidal particles envisaged developing of terbium(III)-based glyphosate sensor with superior LOD and remarkable temperature sensing. The main structural features affecting the sensing of both temperature changes and glyphosate concentration are discussed. The effect of the interfering substrates is also represented.

The synthesis of the tetra- (4a,b) and bis-1,3-diketones (9) of calix[4]arene in the 1,3-alternate isomer form has been performed according to Fig. 1. At the first stage, the 1,3-alternate calix[4]arene tetrabutyl ethers 2a and 2b were prepared using a slightly modified method for tetraalkylation of the p-H-calix[4]arene 1 [30]. The reaction of calix[4]arene 1 [31] with n-alkyl tosylate was carried out in DMF in the presence of an excess of Cs2CO3 as a strong base resulted in desired ethers 2a and 2b with 38% and 59% yields. The dibromocalix[4]arene tetrabutyl ether 7 has been prepared in a similar manner using dibromocalix[4]arene dibutyl ether 6 [32] . The interaction of products 2a, 2b and 7 with methyl chloromethyl ether in the presence of SnCl4 led to the chloromethylated calix[4]arene derivatives 3a,b and 8. The tetra-1,3-diketones 4a,b and 9 were obtained after addition of a sodium acetylacetonate (NaAA) to the compounds 3a,b and 8 and heating the reaction mixtures up to 60 0С, with the yields of reactions being 60%, 49% and 68%, respectively. The synthesis has been performed in the conditions similar to the Finkelstein reaction (halogen exchange in alkyl halides) - in the mixture of acetone and DMF (2:1) in the presence of an excess NaI. In this case, the reaction of nucleophilic substitution proceeds at the lower temperature, which allows to avoid the cleaving of 1,3-diketone groups [17,23]. NMR, IR spectroscopy (Figs. S1 and S2, Table S1), elemental analysis and MALDI-TOF data for the new synthesized compounds are in accordance with their molecular formulae.

IR spectra of the tetra-1,3-diketone derivatives 4a and 4b (Fig. S2) are characterized by presence of doublet absorption bands ν(С=О) (1722, 1698 cm−1 and 1726, 1701 cm−1, correspondingly). A doublet shape of keto-carbonyl absorption bands with almost equal intensities of the peaks indicates a nearly equal ratio of cis- and trans-isomers for the keto-forms of the compounds 4a and 4b. The carbonyl bands (1713 and 1702 cm−1) in the spectrum of the compound 9 are less intense, than those in spectrum of the compounds 4a and 4b, which correlates with the decreased number of 1,3-diketone groups at the upper rim of calix[4]arene 9. A broad intensive band in the region of 1600 cm−1 registered for all 1,3-diketones can be assigned to the ν(С=С) stretching vibrations of multiple aromatic bonds of the macrocycle as well as to the vibrations of the enolic structures. A high intensity of this band testifies obviously a noticeable amount of the enolic structure for these compounds.

For all new synthesized diketones 4a, 4b and 9 as well as their parent alkyl substituted compounds 2a, b 3a,b, 7 and 8, only single 1H NМR signals for -CH2- groups (H9) were observed in the region of 3.54–3.64 ppm (See Table 1). In addition, the signals of their bridged carbon atoms (C9) in 13С NMR spectra are within 36.2–37.8 ppm, that in accordance with the Mendoza rule (δ(C9) ≈ 28–33 ppm for cone and 35–39 ppm for 1,3-alternate isomer) [33] indicates the 1,3-alternate calix[4]arene form for these compounds.

The presence of several 1,3-diketone groups in the molecules of calix[4]arenes 4a, 4b and 9 leads to the complication of NMR spectra for these derivatives due to the realization of several keto-enolic forms in their solutions. The percentages of enolic form for the calix[4]arenes 4a (42%) and 9 (45%) are almost the same as for their cone analog 10a (50%) [16]. However, an introduction of long alkyl substituents, as in the case of 1,3-alternate (4b) and cone derivatives (10b), leads to the decreased amount of the enolic form (30% for 4b and 33% for 10b) [17]. The steric hindrances and lower flexibility of macrocyclic backbone are the reasons for the destabilization of enolic form.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Complex formation of the calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones with Tb3+ ions in the DMF solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The complex formation of the ligands with Tb3+ ions has been studied in DMF solutions, since the latter is the convenient medium for both initial ligands and their terbium complexes. Moreover, DMF solutions are miscible with aqueous ones which facilitates the use of the so-called solvent-exchange procedure for conversion of the terbium complexes into the aqueous colloids. Similar with their cone analogs 10a,b [16,17] UV-Vis absorption spectra of the calix[4]arenes 4a, 4b and 9 (Fig. 1) reveal both intensive absorption band with the maximum at ~ 280 nm attributed to the π-π*electron transitions in aromatic fragments and shoulder in the region ~ 310 nm caused by the absorption of 1,3-diketone enolic forms. Thus, the complex formation undergoing through the 1,3-diketone→1,3-diketonate conversion is well manifested by the changes in the intraligand electronic absorbance spectra. In accordance with the previous reports on the complex formation of 1,3-diketone cone calix[4]arenes with Tb3+ ions is negligible in neutral conditions, being facilitated by triethylamine (TEA) [6,7]. It is worth noting that equimolar amounts of Tb3+ ions generate detectable spectral changes of 4a, 4b and 9 even without basification (Fig. S3), which indicates a possibility of Tb3+-induced keto-enol equilibrium shifting even without TEA.

The Job plotting analysis based on Aexp-ALCL/(CL+CTb) value (AL and Aexp are the absorbance values measured in the basified by TEA solutions of the ligand itself and in the mixture with Tb3+ ions) vs CL/(CL+CTb) plots ( Fig. 2) reveals the predominance of the 1:1 (Tb:L) complex stoichiometry for ligands 4a and 10b. It is worth noting that the Aexp-ALCL/(CL+CTb) values of ligands 4b and 9 are significantly lower than those of ligands 4a and 10b (Fig. 2a). This amplifies the evidence of the lower complex formation constants for the former (even taking into account half the number of 1,3-diketo groups in 9) vs the latter couple of ligands. Moreover, the Job plots of ligand 4b and 9 are characterized by small deviations between the Aexp-ALCL/(CL+CTb) values at CL/(CL+CTb) equal to 0.33, 0.5 and 0.67, which may derive from the interference of the complexes with 1:1, 2:1 and 1:2 stoichiometry (Fig. 2a).

The number of 1,3-diketone groups undergoing Tb3+-induced deprotonation of ligands 4a, 4b, 9 and 10b can be evaluated through the titration curves (Fig. 2b–e) demonstrating the dependence of ΔA (ΔA is the absorbance enhancement) under the increase in TEA:L (L is the ligand) concentration ratio vs the TEA:L ratio. The results (Fig. 2d) reveal that the complex formation of 9 is accompanied by the single 1,3-diketone group deprotonation, while the complex formation of both other 1,3-alternate calix[4]arenes and their cone counterpart occurs through the deprotonation of two 1,3-diketone groups (Fig. 2b, c and e). This fact explains the abovementioned difference between the Aexp-ALCL/(CL+CTb) values of ligands 9 and 4a (Fig. 2a). The slope of ΔA vs TEA:L ratio depends on the substituents of 1,3-alternate calix[4]arenes, being the greatest for 4a and the smallest for 4b (Fig. 2b and c), thus, indicating that the complex formation constant is greater for 4a vs its analog 4b bearing longer alkyl substituents.

The contribution of the complexes with either 2:2 or 2:1 (Tb:L) stoichiometry can be revealed by the diffusion H1 NMR spectroscopy. The decrease in self-diffusion coefficients D s by 20–30% of ligands 4a and 9 confirms a participation of two ligand molecules in the complex formation with Tb3+ ions (Table S2). The dimeric complex formation of 4a through two 1,3-diketonate groups can derive from the sandwich-like coordination of two Tb3+ ions via two 1,3-diketonates between the two ligands, resulting in the 2:2 stoichiometry. The dimeric complex formation of ligand 9 undergoing through the single 1,3-diketonate group can be explained by the predominance of the 1:2 stoichiometry even at the 1:1 (Tb:L) concentration ratio . Moreover, the lack of the clearly defined maximum on the Job plot for ligand 9 (Fig. 2a) amplifies an opportunity of equilibration between complexes of different stoichiometry. Thus, the complex formation of ligands 4a and 4b results in the 2:2 (Tb:L) complexes, although due to the above mentioned difference in their complex formation the admixture of the unbound ligand is greater for the latter vs former ligand, while the complex formation of 9 is contributed by the complexes with 1:1, 1:2 and 2:2 stoichiometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational modeling of the Tb3+complexes with the 1,3-alternate calix[4]arenes
ce:section.ce:para:
In light of above represented experimental results ligand 4a is the leading from the viewpoint of its complex formation with Tb3+ ions. Therefore, the DFT calculations aimed at correlation of the complexes stabilities with their stoichiometry and coordination modes has been performed for ligand 4a. It is worth noting that 2:2 complex stoichiometry has been estimated by the UV-Vis and NMR methods as the predominant complex form for the Tb3+ complexes with ligand 4a, although dimeric and/or dinuclear complex formation commonly undergoes through their mono-counterparts. Thus, Fig. 3 represents the possible complex structures (I-V) with both 1:1, 1:2 and 2:2 stoichiometry formed via the equilibriums (1–8). The calculated thermochemical parameters of the complexes (I-V) from [Tb(H2O)8]3+ and ligand 4а (H4L) in the presence of TEA are collected in Table 1, the optimized coordinates of atoms for ligand 4a and its Tb3+ complexes are presented in SI.
(1) [Tb(H2O)8]3+ + H4L + Et3N ⇆ [TbH3L(H2O)5]2+-(I) + 3H2O + Et3NH+
(2) [Tb(H2O)8]3+ + H4L + 2Et3N ⇆ [TbH2L (H2O)4]+-(II) + 4H2O + 2Et3NH+
(3) [TbH3L(H2O)5]2+-(I) + Et3N ⇆ [TbH2L (H2O)4]+-(II) + H2O + Et3NH+
(4) [Tb(H2O)8]3+ + 2H4L + 4Et3N ⇆ [Tb(H2L)2]--(III) + 8H2O + 4Et3NH+
(5) 2[Tb(H2O)8]3+ + 2H4L + 4Et3N ⇆ [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+-(IV,V) + 8H2O + 4Et3NH+
(6) 2[TbH2L(H2O)4]+(II) ⇆ [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+-(IV, V)
(7) [Tb(H2O)8]3+ + [Tb(H2L)2]-(III) ⇆ [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+-(IV,V)
(8) [TbH2L(H2O)4]+(II) + H4L + 2Et3N⇆ [Tb(H2L)2]--(III) + 4H2O + 2Et3NH+

The thermochemical parameters of formation of [TbH3L(H2O)5]2+-(I) and [TbH2L(H2O)4]+-(II) complexes via equilibriums (1) and (2) (Table 1) clearly reveal complex [TbH3L(H2O)5]2+-(I) as the interim to complex II. This tendency is confirmed by the significant decrease in Gibbs energy (ΔG°298 = − 87.8 kJ in Table 1) under conversion of [TbH3L(H2O)5]2+-(I) into [TbH2L(H2O)4]+-(II) through the equilibrium (3). Thus, complex II is the most favorable form of the 1:1 complexes.

The formation of complex III and complexes IV and V with 1:2 and 2:2 stoichiometry undergoes through equilibriums (4) and (5) correspondingly, and their thermochemical parameters are also represented in Table 1. Comparative analysis of the parameters (Table 1) highlights complex IV as the most favorable structure of [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+, although the thermochemical parameters of IV and V are close to each other (Table 1). Thus, both structures (IV and V) of [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complex can be equilibrated in the DMF solutions.

The dimerization of [TbH2L(H2O)4]+-(II) via equilibrium (6) into [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+-(IV, V) is endothermic process undergoing through the entropy gain (Table 1), which allows to assume the dimerization as kinetically retarded process. The transformation of complex [Tb(H2L)2]--III into [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+-(IV, V) through equilibrium (7) is energetically favorable, which explains the predominance of 2:2 over 1:2 stoichiometry for complexes with ligand 4a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence of the Tb3+complexes in the basified DMF solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complexes with 4a, 4b and 9 in the basified DMF solutions exhibit the Tb3+-centered luminescence [13] manifested by the peculiar spectral pattern arisen from 5D4→7F6 (491 nm), 5D4→7F5 (545 nm), 5D4→7F4 (586 nm), 5D4→7F3 (622 nm) transitions. The intensity ratios of the bands at 489 nm (5D4→7F6) and 545 nm (5D4→7F5) are about 0.3 for the complexes with the 1,3-alternate calix[4]arenes ( Fig. 4). These ratios are close to the ratio (0.3) for [TbH2L(H2O)4]+ complex with cone calix[4]arene 10b, where the complex formation results from intramolecular bis-chelation of Tb3+ ions with two 1,3-diketonate groups. The aforesaid similarity confirms that dimeric terbium complexes with 4a, 4b and 9 derive from the sandwich-like bis-chelating coordination mode of two Tb3+ ions.

The steady state luminescence of the terbium complexes depends on both isomeric form and substituents of the calix[4]arenes (Fig. 4). The luminescence intensity of the basified DMF solutions of the ligands mixed with Tb3+ ions is affected by several factors, which are: (1) the complex formation extent dependent on the equilibrium constant, (2) the lifetime value of the excited state sensitive to radiationless energy losses, (3) an efficacy of the ligand-to-Tb3+ energy transfer, in turn, controlled by the energy gap between the Tb3+-centered excited and ligand-centered triplet energy states. The measured average excited state lifetime values (<τ>) and the triplet energy states of the ligands determined through the luminescence measurements of their gadolinium complexes (for more details see the Exp. Section) are collected in Table 2. The analysis of the results (Table 2, Fig. 4) indicates that the lower luminescence intensity of the complex with 10b vs its 1,3-alternate counterparts (4a, 4b, 9) derives from the lower triplet energy for the former vs the latter ligands, since the energy back transfer [34,35] is more efficient in the case of the lower triplet energy level of 10b vs higher energy levels of 4a, 4b, 9, while the <τ>-value is the greatest for the ligand in cone-isomeric form. Both intensity of the steady state luminescence (Fig. 4) and <τ> values (Table 2) highlight the terbium complex with ligand 4a as the leader among the complexes with the 1,3-alternate ligands. The luminescence intensity of [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complex with 4a reaches the maximum value with increasing of ligand:Tb3+ concentration ratios up to 1:1, being invariant up to 2:1 (Fig. 4c). This fact confirms the enough stability of [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complex with ligand 4a, which shows an advantage of the ligand among other the 1,3-alternate ligands.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conversion of the complexes from the basified DMF solutions into the luminescent aqueous colloids
ce:section.ce:para: Conversion of the terbium complexes into the aqueous PSS-stabilized colloids has been performed through the previously reported solvent-exchange procedure based on the precipitation of Tb3+ complexes from water miscible organic solvent (DMF) to polyelectrolyte-containing (PSS) water solution under effective stirring (for more details see the Exp. Section). This procedure was successfully applied for synthesis of PSS-stabilized nanoparticles based on [TbL] complexes with 10c [24].

The average hydrodynamic diameters of as synthesized aqueous PSS-colloids examined with the use of dynamic light scattering method are collected in Table 3. The size-values revealed in the aqueous PSS-stabilized colloids based on the terbium complexes with 4a and 9 are below 100 nm, while the colloids based on 4b manifest itself by the huge agglomerates (Table 3). It is worth assuming the coprecipitation of both unbound ligand 4b bearing long alkyl substituents and its terbium complex as the reason for the enhanced aggregation. The colloids formed from the complexes with the cone-counterpart 10b also bearing long hydrophobic substituents are characterized by the average size smaller than that for 4b, but greater than that for 4a and 9. Moreover, the colloids based on 10b suffer from the instability manifested by the irreversible precipitation under the storage. The above-mentioned drawbacks of the colloids based on 4b and 10b can be explained by the presence of the surface exposed hydrophobic substituents, which provide a driving force of further agglomeration of the precipitated colloidal species. It is worth noting the previous report [36] for highlighting that the self-aggregation of the terbium complexes with 10b in the aqueous basified DMF solutions of PSS undergoes through the preliminary micellization step, where the hydrophobic tails and hydrophilic Tb3+-bound rim of the complexes are predominantly localized in core and interfacial zones correspondingly. However, such micellization is very quickly followed by the formation of the hard colloids, where the hydrophobic substituents can be either inside or at the surface of the colloidal species. Colloidal species formed from the complexes with 4a and 9 constitute of compact and rather uniform (PDI is 0.250 and 0.222) particles with diameters of 99.9 and 75.3 nm., correspondingly. Zeta potential values for each of studied PSS-stabilized nanoparticles lie within the range of − 28 to − 36 mV, thanks to negatively charged PSS coating (Table 3).

TEM microphotographs ( Fig. 5) reveal the peculiarities of intercolloidal liaison in dry state for PSS-colloids based on [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ of ligands 4a and 4b. The TEM image of the dried colloids for 4a reveals the colloidal particles which retain their individuality manifested by rather delineated quasi spherical shape with contrast borders (Fig. 5a), while that for 4b indicate three dimensional networks of intertwined particles with blurred lined sponge-like structures (Fig. 5b). The specificity in the aggregation behavior confirms the aforesaid assumption of the surface exposed hydrophobic substituents as the reason for the aggregation leading to voluminous 1126.2 ± 350.6 nm agglomerates defined by both TEM in the dried state and DLS in water solution (Table 3). The TEM image of the colloids for 9 is close to that for 4a (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence of the Tb3+complexes in the PSS-stabilized aqueous colloids
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of the intensity ratios at 489 nm (5D4→7F6) and 545 nm (5D4→7F5) of the studied complexes in the PSS-colloids (Fig. 5d) reveals the greater values (about 0.4) than those for the complexes in the DMF solutions (about 0.3), which can be explained by the difference in the inner-sphere environment of the Tb3+ ions due to the substitution of the inner-sphere DMF molecules by water (Table 3). The excited state lifetime values measured in the PSS-colloids are significantly lower than those of the complexes in DMF (Table 3), which also confirms the inner-sphere equation under conversion of the Tb3+ complexes from the DMF solutions to the aqueous PSS-colloids. The luminescence intensities of the PSS-stabilized colloids follow the similar dependence on the ligands nature as the complexes in the basified DMF solutions, being the greater for ligand 4a vs 10b. This confirms safe conversion of [TbH2L(H2O)4]+ and [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complexes with ligands 4a and 10b correspondingly into the PSS-stabilized colloids. The equilibriums between ligand 4b and its terbium complex, as well as between complexes of different stoichiometry in the case of ligand 9 can be shifted under their conversion into the PSS-colloids through the solvent exchange step. Nevertheless, the luminescence of the PSS-colloids derived from ligand 4b is lower than that of the 4a-based colloids (Fig. 5d), while the <τ>values of the colloids are rather similar (Table 3). Moreover, the intraligand emission at 400–450 nm of the unbound ligand becomes evident from the luminescence spectra of the 4b-based colloids under their dilution from 0.75 mM to 0.0075 mM (Fig. S5). This confirms the assumption that the nanoprecipitation of the terbium complexes with 4b occurs along with the unbound ligand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature sensitive luminescence of the Tb3+ complexes in the DMF solutions and in the PSS-colloids
ce:section.ce:para: The so-called temperature-induced back energy transfer is the main mechanism responsible for the temperature-dependent luminescence of Tb3+ complexes, although the other heating-induced factors, such as radiationless losses through the enhanced intra-ligand molecular motion and shifting of complex formation equilibriums can also contribute to the heating-induced quenching. The latter factor explains the insufficient reversibility of luminescence under the heating/cooling of the complexes with ligands 4b and 9 in the DMF solutions ( Fig. 6a,b). The normalized luminescence intensity of [TbH2L(H2O)4]+ complex with 10b plotted vs temperature also deviates from linearity (Fig. 6c), which differentiates [TbH2L(H2O)4]+ complex with 10b from its structural counterpart with ligand 10c.

The temperature sensitivity (S
I ) values calculated through the Eq. (9) for the complexes with 10b and 10c at 323 K are − 4.79%·K and −7.68%·K [24] correspondingly.
(9)
S
I = ΔI/(I ΔT)·100%,
where ΔI is the change in the luminescence intensity with the temperature variation ΔT and I is the luminescence intensity at a given temperature. It is worth assuming the heating-induced aggregation of the terbium complexes with 10b as the reason for the restricted linearity of the temperature dependence of the normalized luminescence and the SI value. Moreover, the S
I -value of the terbium complexes with ligand 9 at 323 K (− 2.32%·K) is surpassed by the values of 4a and 4b, which are − 5.12%·K and − 5.17%·K at 323 K, correspondingly. The aforesaid structure impact of the ligands on the thermal sensitivity of their terbium complexes correlates with the specific complex formation of ligand 9 resulting in the complexes with different stoichiometry equilibrated with each other in the DMF solutions. Moreover, S
I reaches greater value for [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complex of 4a (− 5.12) than for [TbH2L(H2O)4]+ of 10b (− 4.79), although this fact doesn’t correlate with the energy gap between triplet levels of the ligands and resonant 5D4 level of Tb3+ ion (~ 20500 cm−1), since the energy level of the triplet state is lower for 10b vs 4a (Table 2). The luminescence decay measurements before and after the heating to 323 K indicate that <τ> values remain practically unchanged under the heating and cooling, thus, excluding the contribution of the radiationless losses to the heating-induced quenching of [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complex with 4a (Table 2). Both reversibility of temperature-induced changes and greatest temperature sensitivity of [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complex with 4a in the DMF solutions is the reason for choosing it as the leader for further studies of its temperature-sensitive behavior in the aqueous PSS-stabilized colloids.

The complex [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ with 4a remains its temperature sensitivity after the conversion into the PSS-colloids. Moreover, the S
I -value of [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ exhibits less pronounced decrease from − 5.12 to − 4.67 under its conversion from DMF solutions to the PSS-colloids (Fig. 6d and f), while the S
I -value of [TbH2L(H2O)4]+ complex with 10c exhibits nearly two-fold decrease after the conversion into the PSS-colloids [24]. The decrease in the S
I value can derive from both inner- and outer-sphere hydration effects on the ligand-to-Tb3+ energy transfer. However, the nearly two fold decrease in <τ> values of [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ with ligand 4a in the PSS-colloids under their heating to 323 K (Table 3) indicates that the heating-induced luminescence quenching is significantly contributed by the enhanced radiationless losses. Thus, the comparative analysis of both reversibility and sensitivity of the terbium complexes with 1,3-diketone calix[4]arene-based ligands in the DMF solutions and after conversion into the PSS-colloids reveals [Tb2(H2L)2(H2O)8]2+ complex with H4L = 4a as the leader among the other studied ligands.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent sensing of glyphosate in aqueous solutions by the Tb3+ complexes
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of various glyphosate (Gph) concentrations on the luminescence of the PSS- colloids from the Tb3+ complexes with ligands 4a, 4b, 9 and 10 was measured at various concentration conditions in order to reveal the optimal ligands structure and conditions for the greatest sensitivity. The structure effect is evident from the lack or insufficient sensitivity of the luminescence response of the PSS-colloids based on ligands 10b and 4b (Figs. S5 and 6), while the concentration-induced quenching of the Tb3+-centered luminescence is observed in the PSS-colloids built from ligands 4a and 9 ( Fig. 7a and c). The quenching effect amplifies the complexation of Gph with Tb3+ ions as the reason for their stripping from the binding sites of ligands 4a and 9. The negligible stripping of Tb3+ ions from the complexes of ligands 10b and 4b can derive from the steric hindrance of the interfacial Tb3+ ions by the surface exposed hydrophobic substituents. Comparative analysis of the Gph-induced quenching (Fig. 7b and d) reveals the terbium complexes with ligand 4a as more sensitive sensor for Gph than those with ligand 9. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated as the concentration of glyphosate enough to trigger the analytical response equal to three standard deviations of the background luminescence intensity for the colloids based on 4a. The monitoring of the Tb3+-centered luminescence in the Tris buffer at pH = 7.12 provides more sensitive response on Gph than the sensing in pure water, since the complexing ability of Gph is pH-dependent. Thus, further optimization of the sensing conditions through adjusting the optimal pH (7.12) by the TRIS-buffer system has been performed for the PSS-colloids based on 4a, which enables to decrease the LOD-value from 498 nM (Fig. 7c) to 1.97 nM (Fig. 7d).

The repeatability was established for nine independent analyses of sample solutions containing different amounts of Gph, obtaining relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 3%. A comparison with other analytical techniques of Gph luminescent detection was carried out and represented in Table 4. The sensitivity of the proposed sensor based on the terbium complexes with 4a under their conversion into the PSS-colloids is substantially higher than majority of other analytical methods, 1.97 nM versus 41–790 nM. Two multicomponent sensoric systems, based on gold nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots in DNA dispersions referenced as [37] and [38] in Table 4 discover lower subnanomolar LODs, 0.27 nM and 0.058 nM, correspondingly. However, costly materials and sophisticated structural composition significantly restricts wide implementation in everyday use. This fact, along with the synthesis, makes the suggested technique promising alternative to other existing methods of glyphosate luminescent determination in water samples.

Evaluation of the sensing selectivity requires the proper choice of interfering substrates, in turn, affected by the structure of the centers giving the luminescent response. In particular, a lack or poor interfering effect is anticipated for the molecules with poor chelating ability. This enables to limit the series of the interfering substrates by carbonic and carboxylic acids, exemplified by hydrocarbonate, glycine, alanine and phthalic acid. The results presented in Table S4 demonstrate that the luminescence response quantitatively evaluated by I/I0 values to the hundred-fold excess amounts of the aforesaid chelating compounds is no more than 6%, which hardly exceeds the standard deviation.

It is worth noting that practical applicability of any analytical technique can be evaluated through quantitation limit (called also as limit of quantification (LOQ)) of the method calculated as ten standard deviations of the background luminescence intensity. The LOQ-value of the developed luminescent technique equals to 2.5 μg/kg, which is 10 times lower than the lowest prescription (25 μg/kg) designated by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA), since the maximum residue limit (MRL) of glyphosate prescribed by EFSA ranges from 25 to 2000 μg/kg depending on the type of product (European Food Safety Authority, 2015). Thus, this strategy delivers fresh and innovative insight for Gph detection in water solutions at nanomolar range, which can be further applied in food, crops, and water quality control.

The series of newly synthesized 1,3-diketone calix[4]arenes in 1,3-alternate isomeric form with buthyl, dodecyl and bromine substituents is represented as alternative ligands (L) for the previously reported cone isomer of nonyl-substituted calix[4]arene 1,3-diketone. The complex formation stoichiometry determined by UV-Vis and diffusion H1 NMR spectroscopy methods in the basified DMF solutions is greatly affected by both isomeric forms and nature of substituents in 1,3-diketone calix[4]arenes. The 1:1 (Tb:L) complex derived from the intramolecular bis-chelation of Tb3+ ion by the cone-isomer of 1,3-diketone calix[4]arene changes to the 2:2 complex formation resulted from the intermolecular bis-chelation of two Tb3+ ions by two 1,3-alternate-counterparts. The DFT calculations reveal the 2:2 complex formation as the thermodynamically favorable for the 1,3-alternate ligands. However, the experimental results indicate that the 2:2 complex formation is restricted for both dodecyl and bromo-substituted ligands, resulting in shifting of the complex formation equilibrium to the unbound ligand and to complexes with 1:2 stoichiometry for the dodecyl and bromo-substituted ligands correspondingly. The 2:2 complexes with buthyl-substituted 1,3-alternate ligand exhibit the most intense luminescence among the terbium complexes with the other ligands in the DMF solutions. The one-pot conversion of the produced complexes into the PSS-colloids is represented as the facile route for their sensing application. The “structure-property” analysis of the aggregation behavior of the PSS-colloids based on the differently substituted ligands highlights the disadvantage of the dodecyl- and nonyl-substituted ligands, since the surface exposed hydrophobic substituents enhance the aggregation of the colloid species. The sensing properties of the complexes converted into the PSS-colloids are also greatly affected by both isomeric forms of calix[4]arene 1,3-diketones and nature of their substituents. This allows to highlight 1,3-alternate of buthyl-substituted as the optimal ligand for 2:2 terbium complexes which being converted into the PSS-colloids exhibit the high luminescence with the greatest sensitivity to both widespread pollutant glyphosate with LOD = 1.97 nM and temperature changes within the physiological temperature range with SI = − 4.67 K-% at 323 K. The revealed “structure-property” relationships open a window for rational structure design of inner- and outer-sphere environment of lanthanide centers for development of the nanosensors with dual modality exemplified in the present work by the luminescence sensitivity to both temperature changes and presence of glyphosate.

Acetylacetone was distilled before use. Methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK) (Acros Organics) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (Acros Organics) was distilled over P2O5. CDCl3 (99.8% isotopic purity) and DMSO-d 6 (99.5% isotopic purity) from Aldrich were used for NMR spectroscopy. Triethylamine, n-butyl tosylate, poly(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) (MWaverage = 70.000), sodium chloride (NaCl) (Acros Organics), Glyphosate (Sigma-Aldrich) and terbium nitrate (Tb(NO3)3·xH2O) (Alfa Aesar) were used as commercially received without further purification. The solution of chloromethyl methyl ether (6.5 M/l) [17] and NaAA [46] were synthesized as described elsewhere.

Synthesis: The synthetic routes, the structural formulae and numbering of atoms of the investigated compounds are shown in Fig. 1. The calix[4]arenes 1 [31], 5 [47], 6 [32], 10a [16] and 10b [17] were obtained as described in literature. 1H and 13C chemical shifts and spin-spin coupling constants observed for new synthesized tetrathiacalix[4]arenes 2b, 3a,b, 4a,b, 8 and 9 are presented in Table S1.

Conversion of the Tb
3+
complexes from basified DMF to PSS-stabilized aqueous colloids was as following: a solution of the terbium salt Tb(NO3)3 was mixed with ligand L in equal proportions with the addition of six equivalents of the deprotonating agent triethylamine to facilitate the elimination of the hydrogen cation from 1,3-diketone groups. The resulting solution of the terbium complex in DMF (C = 4.5 mM) was added dropwise to an aqueous solution of PSS (C = 1 g/L) and sodium chloride (C = 0.5 M) under vigorous stirring. In this case, the PSS molecules, due to the charge interaction, are wound on the formed, water-insoluble templates from the terbium complexes and stabilize them in the aqueous medium.

UV–visible spectrophotometry data were obtained using a Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Studies of the fluorescence of TbL complexes in a DMF solution and in the composition of nanoparticles, their sensitivity to the presence of glyphosate, the temperature sensitivity of fluorescence were carried out using a Hitachi F-7100 spectrofluorometer (Japan) with stigmatic concave diffraction grating. The lifetimes of the excited state of TbL complexes were studied using Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog III spectrofluorimeter. Colloidal characteristics of nanoparticles (hydrodynamic diameter, polydispersity index, electrokinetic potential) were obtained using Anton Paar Litesizer 500 particle analyzer. Each measurement was performed three or more times until a reproducible result was obtained with a difference in absolute values of less than 4%. Transmission electron microscopy images were obtained using a Hitachi HT7700 microscope using a copper mesh substrate with a formvar support film.

Quantum-chemical modeling of the Tb3+ complex structures with 4a (H4L) was carried out using the PRIRODA program package [50] within the density functional theory employing the generalized gradient approximation PBE functional [51]. For all atoms, a specially optimized double-zeta atomic basis set L1 (similar to cc-pVDZ) by Laikov [52] was applied. The PBE functional has demonstrated its reliability in our early theoretical studies of a number of supramolecular systems [53–58].

All computations were performed with full gas-phase optimization of molecular geometry without any symmetry constraints. Optimized coordinates of atoms for ligand 4a and its Tb3+ complexes are available in Supplementary. The geometry optimization was followed by calculations of vibrational spectra. Normal mode vibrational frequencies were calculated in the harmonic approximation without using any scaling factors. The absence of imaginary values in the frequency spectrum of normal modes was deemed to confirm that the obtained structures correspond to the minimum on the total potential energy surface. On the basis of thermochemical analysis (at 298 K temperature and 1 Atm pressure) the total entropy, enthalpy and Gibbs free energy of each system were obtained.

Since the PRIRODA program package does not provide for the possibility of carrying out quantum-chemical calculations taking into account the influence of a solvent, we used the following approach to evaluation of solvent effects. Using a fixed geometry of particles involved in the reaction, previously optimized with the PRIRODA program, their total energy in the gas phase and in solution was calculated using the Gaussian09 [59] program package within the Polarizable Continuum Model (PCM) [60–62]. The solvation energy of a particle was calculated as the difference between the total energy in the gas phase and in solution, and then it was added to the total gas-phase energy of the particle obtained with the PRIRODA program package. Since the atomic basis set L1 is not implemented in the Gaussian09 basis set library, we used for all the atoms the x2c-SVPAll basis set of a similar size [63].

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rustem R. Zairov was born in 1983. Graduated from Kazan Federal university in 2005. Received his PhD in inorganic chemistry in 2009. Senior scientific researcher of Laboratory of supramolecular systems A.E. Arbuzov IOPC KSC RAS. Associate professor of Physical chemistry department of Kazan Federal University. Member of IAAM, IACIS, NFGL, ACS. H = 13 (Scopus).
Alexey P. Dovzhenko was born in 1997. Graduated from Kazan Federal university in 2020. PhD student of physical chemistry department of Kazan Federal University. H = 2 (Scopus).

Sergey N. Podyachev was born in 1967. Graduated from Kazan Federal university in 1989. Received his PhD in physical chemistry in 1995. Senior scientific researcher of Laboratory of chemistry and physics of supramolecules A.E. Arbuzov IOPC KSC RAS. H= 14 (Scopus).

Svetlana N. Sudakova was born in 1961. Graduated from Kazan Federal university in 2005. Received his PhD in organic chemistry in 1987. Senior scientific researcher of Laboratory of supramolecular systems A.E. Arbuzov IOPC KSC RAS. H = 12 (Scopus).

Alexey N. Masliy was born in 1973. Received his Ph.D. degree in electochemistry in 1999. Associate professor of department of Inorganic Chemistry of Kazan National Reseach Technological Unoversity. H = 10 (Scopus).

Irek R. Nizameev was born in 1986. Graduated from Kazan Federal university in 2009. Received his PhD in physical chemistry in 2013. Scientific researcher of Electrochemical Synthesis Laboratory of A.E. Arbuzov IOPC KSC RAS. Associate professor of department of nanotechnology in electronics of Kazan National Research Technical University named after A.N. Tupolev - KAI. H = 17 (Scopus).

Yulia H. Budnikova was born in 1965. Received her doctorate degree in physical chemistry in 1999. The head of Head of Electrochemical Synthesis Laboratory of A.E. Arbuzov IOPC KSC RAS. H = 27 (Scopus).

Andrey M. Kuznetsov was born in 1955. Received his doctorate degree in electochemistry in 1992. Head of department of Inorganic Chemistry of Kazan National Reseach Technological Unoversity. H = 14 (Scopus).

Oleg G. Sinyashin was born in 1956. Received his doctorate degree in organic chemistry in 1990. Academician of RAS since 2006. The head of A.E. Arbuzov IOPC KSC RAS (2001–2017). The head of chemistry department of Kazan National Technological university (2001-present time). H = 33 (Scopus).
Asiya R. Mustafina was born in 1961. Received her doctorate degree in physical chemistry in 2008. The head of Laboratory of supramolecular systems A.E. Arbuzov IOPC KSC RAS. H = 21 (Scopus).
